section.title: Wisdom Builds, Folly Destroys
section.sectionRationale: One of the longest chapters in the collection, gathering sayings on building and tearing down, verses 1 to 7, folly's self deception and the way that seems right, verses 8 to 14, prudence and kindness to the poor, verses 15 to 21, honest labor and the fear of the LORD as a fountain of life, verses 22 to 28, and patience, a tranquil heart, and a nation's true exaltation, verses 29 to 35.
section.thesis: Wisdom quietly builds a house, a life, and a nation, while folly, often through paths that feel entirely right from the inside, just as quietly tears them down, and only the fear of the LORD reliably tells the two apart.
section.frameNote: Five thematic clusters of seven verses each: building and tearing down, verses 1 to 7; folly's self deception and the way that seems right, verses 8 to 14; prudence and kindness to the poor, verses 15 to 21; honest labor and the fear of the LORD as a fountain of life, verses 22 to 28; and patience, a tranquil heart, and a nation's true exaltation, verses 29 to 35.

verses.text: Every wise woman {{banah|builds}} her house, but a foolish one tears it down with her own hands.
He who walks in uprightness fears the LORD, but the one who is devious in his ways despises Him.
The proud speech of a fool brings a rod to his back, but the lips of the wise protect them.
Where there are no oxen, the manger is empty, but an abundant harvest comes through the strength of the ox.
An honest witness does not deceive, but a dishonest witness pours forth lies.
A mocker seeks wisdom and finds none, but knowledge comes easily to the discerning.
Stay away from a foolish man; you will gain no knowledge from his speech.
The wisdom of the prudent is to discern his way, but the folly of fools deceives them.
Fools mock the making of amends, but goodwill is found among the upright.
The heart knows its own bitterness, and no stranger shares in its joy.
The house of the wicked will be destroyed, but the tent of the upright will flourish.
There is a {{derek|way}} that seems right to a man, but its end is the way of death.
Even in laughter the heart may ache, and joy may end in sorrow.
The backslider in heart receives the fill of his own ways, but a good man is rewarded for his ways.

units.label: Prudence and kindness to the poor
units.note: watching your steps and your neighbor

verses.text: The simple man believes every word, but the prudent man watches his steps.
A wise man fears and turns from evil, but a fool is careless and reckless.
A quick-tempered man acts foolishly, and a devious man is hated.
The simple inherit folly, but the prudent are crowned with knowledge.
The evil bow before the good, and the wicked at the gates of the righteous.
The poor man is hated even by his neighbor, but many are those who love the rich.
He who despises his neighbor sins, but blessed is he who shows {{chanan|kindness}} to the poor.

units.label: Honest labor and the fountain of life
units.note: the fear of the LORD, again

verses.text: Do not those who contrive evil go astray? But those who plan goodness find loving devotion and faithfulness.
There is profit in all labor, but mere talk leads only to poverty.
The crown of the wise is their wealth, but the effort of fools is folly.
A truthful witness saves lives, but one who utters lies is deceitful.
He who fears the LORD is secure in confidence, and his children shall have a place of refuge.
The {{yirah|fear of the LORD}} is a fountain of life, turning a man from the snares of death.
A large population is a king's splendor, but a lack of subjects is a prince's ruin.

units.label: Patience, a tranquil heart, and a nation exalted

verses.text: A patient man has great understanding, but a quick-tempered man promotes folly.
A tranquil heart is life to the body, but envy rots the bones.
Whoever oppresses the poor taunts their Maker, but whoever is kind to the needy honors Him.
The wicked man is thrown down by his own sin, but the righteous man has a refuge even in death.
Wisdom rests in the heart of the discerning; even among fools she is known.
Righteousness exalts a nation, but sin is a disgrace to any people.
A king delights in a wise servant, but his anger falls on the shameful.

banah.gloss: To build, used in verse 1 of the wise woman who builds her house, set against the foolish woman who tears hers down with her own hands. The verb pictures wisdom and folly not as abstract qualities but as ongoing, active work with lasting structural consequences.

derek.gloss: Way or path, used in verse 12's warning that a way can seem right to a person and still end in death. The same line reappears nearly word for word at 16:25, making it one of the book's most emphatic warnings against trusting one's own inner sense of rightness above God's revealed wisdom.

chanan.gloss: To show favor, grace, or kindness, used in verse 21 of kindness shown to the poor, called a blessing in deliberate contrast to the sin of despising one's neighbor. The same root describes God's own gracious favor throughout the Old Testament, tying human kindness to the poor to the character of God Himself.

yirah.gloss: The fear of the LORD, called a fountain of life in verse 27, using imagery identical to 13:14's fountain of wise teaching, and tying this whole chapter's many contrasts of wisdom and folly back to the book's foundational reverence for God.

basic.context: This long chapter surveys wisdom and folly across household, workplace, courtroom, and nation. A wise woman builds her house while a foolish one tears hers down with her own hands. A path can feel entirely right and still lead to death. Kindness to the poor honors the very God who made them, while oppressing them is nothing less than taunting their Maker. And a whole nation, not just an individual, is exalted by righteousness or disgraced by sin.
basic.hermeneutics: As elsewhere in this collection, these are independent, largely antithetic proverbs, but this chapter's sheer length allows several themes to be explored from multiple angles, folly's self deception, verses 8 to 14, prudence toward the poor, verses 15 to 21, and the fear of the LORD as life's true foundation, verses 26 to 27. Verse 12's warning is especially important for reading the whole book rightly, a path's felt rightness is not proof of its actual rightness; only revealed wisdom, rooted in the fear of the LORD, can be fully trusted.
basic.originalLanguages: Banah, to build, verse 1, pictures wisdom and folly as ongoing construction or demolition, not static traits. Derek, way, verse 12, names a path that can feel right while still ending in death, a warning repeated nearly verbatim at 16:25. Chanan, to show kindness or favor, verse 21, links kindness toward the poor to the same gracious character elsewhere used of God's own favor. And yirah, the fear of the LORD, verse 27, is called a fountain of life, echoing 13:14's fountain of wise teaching.
basic.typology: Verse 31's claim that oppressing the poor taunts their Maker, while kindness to them honors Him, anticipates Christ's own teaching that whatever is done to the least of these is done to Him personally, Matthew 25:40, 45, showing that God identifies Himself with the poor and needy in a way this proverb had already glimpsed.
basic.christ: Verse 12's warning, that a way can seem right to a person and still end in death, exposes humanity's need for a wisdom beyond its own inner sense of rightness, a need met fully in Christ, who alone is the way, the truth, and the life, John 14:6, the one path that truly leads where it feels it should. And verse 31's claim that kindness to the needy honors God Himself is deepened immeasurably by Christ's own words that whatever is done for the least of His brothers is done for Him, Matthew 25:40, showing that God's identification with the poor is not incidental but personal, ultimately embodied in the incarnate Christ.
basic.god: God is the one whose fear grounds a truly secure life and a fountain of life itself, verses 26 to 27, and He is personally invested in how the poor are treated, taking their mistreatment as an insult to Himself and their care as an honor to Himself, verse 31. He also governs whole nations, not merely individuals, exalting or disgracing them according to righteousness or sin, verse 34.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 16:25 repeats verse 12 nearly word for word, reinforcing its warning. Matthew 25:40, 45 has Christ identify Himself with the poor and needy, echoing verse 31. John 14:6 presents Christ as the way that truly leads to life, the answer to the false way of verse 12. Galatians 6:9 encourages perseverance in doing good, related to the fruit of patient understanding in verse 29.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's picture of wisdom quietly building and folly quietly tearing down, of paths that feel right yet end in death, and of God's own investment in how the poor are treated, belongs to Scripture's ongoing story of the two ways and the God who governs both individuals and nations. That story reaches its resolution in Christ, the one true way to life, who identifies Himself so fully with the poor that kindness to them is kindness to Him.

context.literary: One of the longest chapters in the Solomonic collection, still built from independent antithetic proverbs but showing sustained clustering around several themes across its five sections, self deception, prudence, honest labor, and the exaltation of nations, giving the chapter unusual breadth for a single collection of short sayings.
context.historical: The image of oxen and an empty or full manger, verse 4, reflects the real agricultural calculus of ancient households, where owning working animals meant both cost and far greater productive capacity, and verse 28's concern for a large population as a king's splendor reflects the ancient world's link between population, agricultural labor, and military strength.
context.canonical: Verse 12's warning is repeated almost verbatim at 16:25, an unusual doubling within the same collection that signals its special importance, and verse 31's linking of the poor to their Maker anticipates a theme, God's identification with the vulnerable, that runs through the prophets and reaches its clearest statement in Christ's words in Matthew 25.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press home, through a wide survey of household, workplace, courtroom, and national life, that wisdom's fruit is real even when it builds quietly, and folly's ruin is certain even when its path feels entirely right, so that the reader learns to distrust mere inner conviction and instead root life in the fear of the LORD.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Direct evaluations and commands, stay away from a foolish man, verse 7, show kindness to the poor, verse 21, are prescriptive. Statements of pattern, the tent of the upright will flourish, verse 11, the righteous man has a refuge even in death, verse 32, describe the general, trustworthy shape of a wise versus a foolish life rather than a fixed formula guaranteeing identical, visible outcomes for every individual; verse 12's own warning that a way can 'seem' right without truly being so cautions against reading any single proverb, including promises of prosperity, as an infallible, mechanical rule detached from the book's larger, more nuanced picture.

debates.question: Does verse 12, 'there is a way that seems right to a man,' undermine confidence in human conscience or reasoning altogether?

views.label: A warning specifically against self deceived, ungrounded conviction
views.case: The verse's repetition at 16:25 and its context among warnings about fools and mockers suggests it targets a conscience or reasoning process not submitted to God's revealed wisdom, the fear of the LORD named just a few verses later, verse 27, rather than condemning human reasoning as such.

views.label: A broader caution about the limits and fallibility of unaided human judgment
views.case: Jeremiah 17:9 states plainly that the heart is deceitful above all things, suggesting Proverbs here voices a wider biblical suspicion of human judgment left entirely to itself, not only judgment that is deliberately rebellious.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on the same practical counsel, human judgment, however sincere it feels, requires the correction of revealed wisdom and the fear of the LORD to be trustworthy; the verse is not an argument against reasoning itself but against relying on the mere feeling of rightness as sufficient proof that a path truly leads to life.

study.originalLanguages: Banah, to build, verse 1, frames wisdom and folly as active, ongoing construction or demolition rather than static labels. Derek, way, verse 12, names a path that can feel entirely right while still ending in death, a warning so important it is repeated almost word for word at 16:25. Chanan, kindness or favor, verse 21, links human kindness to the poor with the same root elsewhere used of God's own gracious favor. And yirah, the fear of the LORD, verse 27, is again called a fountain of life, echoing 13:14 and binding this chapter's wide ranging observations back to the book's central motto.

typology.type: A way that seems right to a man but ends in death, Proverbs 14:12
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true way, truth, and life, the one path whose end truly matches its promise, John 14:6.

typology.type: Kindness to the needy honors their Maker, Proverbs 14:31
typology.fulfillment: Christ's identification with the poor, whatever you did for the least of these, you did for Me, Matthew 25:40.

study.christ: Verse 12's sober warning, that a way can feel entirely right to a person and still end in death, exposes exactly the need Christ meets when He declares Himself 'the way, and the truth, and the life,' John 14:6, the one path whose felt rightness and actual end are never at odds because He Himself is both guide and destination. And verse 31's claim that oppressing the poor taunts their Maker while kindness to them honors Him anticipates, almost prophetically, Christ's own words that whatever is done for the least of His brothers and sisters is done for Him personally, Matthew 25:40, 45, showing God's identification with the vulnerable was never incidental but was always moving toward becoming personal and incarnate.
study.god: God grounds a life of true security and is Himself the fountain of life for those who fear Him, verses 26 to 27. He takes the treatment of the poor personally, counting their oppression an insult to Himself and their care an honor to Himself, verse 31, and He governs the exaltation or disgrace of entire nations according to righteousness or sin, verse 34, not merely the fortunes of individuals.

crossReferences.note: There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way of death, repeating verse 12 almost word for word.
Whatever you did for the least of these, you did for Me, echoing and deepening verse 31's link between the poor and their Maker.
Christ as the way, the truth, and the life, the true path whose end matches its promise, answering the false way of verse 12.
The heart is deceitful above all things, a related biblical caution about trusting unaided human judgment, relevant to verse 12's warning.

study.oneStory: This chapter's wide survey of wisdom quietly building and folly quietly destroying, alongside its warning that a path can feel right and still lead to death, and its insistence that God takes the treatment of the poor personally, belongs to Scripture's larger story of the two ways and the God who cares for the vulnerable. That story culminates in Christ, the one true way to life, who so fully identifies with the poor that kindness shown to them is kindness shown to Him.

quiz.q: What image does verse 1 use to describe wisdom and folly in the home?

options.text: A wise woman builds her house, while a foolish one tears it down with her own hands.
options.note: This is the exact image and contrast given.

options.text: Both wise and foolish women build equally strong households.
options.note: The verse draws a sharp contrast, not equal outcomes.

options.text: Only men are responsible for building a household in this proverb.
options.note: The proverb specifically addresses a woman's active role in building or tearing down the house.

options.text: Foolishness only affects the household's finances, not its structure or stability.
options.note: The image of tearing down 'with her own hands' suggests a broader, active destruction, not narrowly financial.

quiz.q: What warning does verse 12 give, and where else does it appear in Proverbs?

options.text: A way can seem right to a person yet end in death, a warning repeated nearly word for word at 16:25.
options.note: This exact repetition signals the warning's special importance.

options.text: Feeling confident about a decision always guarantees it is correct.
options.note: The verse warns precisely against trusting felt rightness as proof of a path's actual safety.

options.text: It appears only once in the entire book, with no repetition.
options.note: The verse is repeated almost verbatim later, at 16:25.

options.text: It is a warning specifically about financial investments.
options.note: The warning is general, about any path in life, not limited to financial decisions.

quiz.q: According to verse 31, what does oppressing the poor amount to?

options.text: Taunting their Maker, while kindness to the needy honors Him.
options.note: This is the exact and striking claim of the verse.

options.text: A private matter with no spiritual significance.
options.note: The verse explicitly ties treatment of the poor to how one treats God Himself.

options.text: Something God is indifferent toward.
options.note: The strong language, taunts their Maker, shows deep divine concern, not indifference.

options.text: A concern only for religious leaders to address.
options.note: The proverb addresses this as a matter for any person, not a specialized religious duty.

quiz.q: What does verse 34 say determines a nation's exaltation or disgrace?

options.text: Righteousness exalts a nation, but sin is a disgrace to any people.
options.note: This is the exact statement of the verse.

options.text: Military strength alone.
options.note: The verse names righteousness and sin, not military power, as decisive.

options.text: The size of a nation's population only.
options.note: Population is mentioned nearby, verse 28, but this specific verse names righteousness and sin as the true determining factors.

options.text: Wealth accumulated by its rulers.
options.note: The verse's concern is moral, righteousness versus sin, not the wealth of rulers.

quiz.q: How does verse 31's link between the poor and their Maker anticipate Christ's teaching?

options.text: Christ teaches that whatever is done for the least of His brothers is done for Him personally, deepening this proverb's claim.
options.note: Matthew 25:40, 45 makes this connection directly and personally in Christ.

options.text: It has no connection to anything Christ later taught.
options.note: Matthew 25 draws on exactly this kind of identification between God and the poor.

options.text: It shows that only the poor themselves are accountable to God.
options.note: The verse addresses how others treat the poor, not a special accountability placed on the poor themselves.

options.text: It applies only to ancient Israelite civil law, with no further relevance.
options.note: This understates how directly the New Testament develops and personalizes this very principle in Christ.

questions: Verse 1 pictures a wise woman actively building her house and a foolish one tearing hers down with her own hands. What are you actively building, or unintentionally tearing down, in your own home or family right now?

Verse 12 warns that a way can feel completely right and still lead to death. Is there a decision or direction in your life that feels right to you but that you have not actually tested against Scripture or wise counsel?

Verse 21 calls kindness to the poor a blessing, and verse 31 says oppressing them taunts God Himself. How does your treatment of those with less than you reflect, or fail to reflect, how you would treat God directly?
Verse 29 says a patient man has great understanding, while a quick-tempered man promotes folly. Where has impatience recently led you to say or do something foolish?
Christ says whatever is done for the least of His brothers is done for Him. Who is someone vulnerable in your life this week that you could serve as though you were serving Christ Himself?

translationNotes.note: This verse is difficult in the Hebrew, and translations vary; the BSB rendering, fools mock the making of amends, captures the general sense of fools despising reconciliation that upright people value.

The phrase 'a refuge even in death' can also be understood as trusting in his integrity even in the face of death, both readings pointing to the righteous having a confidence that outlasts mortal danger.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 14:1-35. No em or en dashes present in this passage; none used anywhere in the file.
